<par>The first syllable of WT <latinform>sgo-pur</latinform> 'foreskin (vulg.)' does not appear to descend from this etymon, but seems rather to mean 'door; aperture, outlet'. However, Jäschke does not include this under compounds with <latinform>sgo</latinform> 'door' (114-6), but rather lists it as a separate head-entry (p. 116). The meaning of the second syllable <latinform>-pur</latinform> is unclear, though there might be a connection with <latinform>ḥp'ur-ba</latinform> 'wrap up, envelop'.</par>

<par>We are provisionally assigning the second syllable of the Namuyi and Shixing forms to this etymon, but the second syllables of Pumi <latinform>ʐɑ¹¹qu⁵⁵</latinform>, <latinform>rɐ³⁵ ku⁵⁵</latinform> to <xref ref="305">#305</xref>, below. The first syllables of all these compounds mean 'fowl', from <xref ref="1606">#1606</xref>.</par>
<par>For the second syllable, see <xref ref="3438">#3438</xref>, above.</par>

<par>The change <latinform>du</latinform> > <latinform>bu</latinform> is regular in Ersu. See Ersu <latinform>bu³³mo⁵⁵</latinform>/Lyuzu (TBL) <latinform>du⁵³mu⁵³</latinform>  'mad person'; Er. <latinform>bu⁵⁵</latinform>/Lz. (Mianning) <latinform>`du</latinform> 'plow'; Er. <latinform>jɑ³³bu⁵⁵</latinform>/Lz. <latinform>`ʒduʒdu</latinform> 'thick'; Er. <latinform>bu⁵⁵tʂhɛ⁵⁵</latinform>/Lz. <latinform>dʐu⁵³tʂhɯ³¹</latinform> 'year'; and Er. <latinform>bu⁵⁵phɑ⁵⁵</latinform>/Lz. (Mianning) <latinform>dʑypʰæ</latinform>  'stomach'. This development is plausibly to be explained in terms of assimilation to the rhyme <latinform>-u</latinform> which is realized as a bilabial trill in this environment (Dominic Yu, p.c.).</par>

<par>The second syllable of Lahu 'mouth' occurs in several other words <latinform>làʔ=tɔ-qɔ</latinform> 'palm of the hand', <latinform>mə̂-<emph>qɔ</emph></latinform> 'lip', <latinform>mû-lɔ̀ʔ-qɔ</latinform> 'daytime; afternoon', and is glossed 'noun formative of elusive meaning'. For now, however, we are assigning it to the present root.</par>
<par>The initial of the second syllable is apparently a rhotacism from d-; cf. Ganan <latinform>kr̀dblquote adɔʔ⁴</latinform> ‘neck’ (164268).</par>
<par>Note that it is probably not plausible to tag this form differently from the Geman form <latinform>go³¹ɹoŋ³⁵</latinform> (39873), which has been tagged as a monomorphemic form 489.</par>
<par>For alternative etymologies, see STC notes 102, 109, 137. The Jingpho word for ‘hand’ (<latinform>lətáʔ</latinform> in the STC’ s notation) may be monomorphemically from *lak or *glak.</par>

<par>Note the assimilation of the medial consonants in rGyalrong <latinform>jɐŋ mki</latinform>.</par>

<par>This form is also cited in DL:852 as <latinform>pɛ̀-pɨ</latinform> ‘crop of bird; (human) breast, chest’. It is co-allofamic with <latinform>pɛ̀-pì(-qu)</latinform> ‘chicken’s breast’ (cf. also <latinform>pɛ̀-qu</latinform> ‘goiter’).</par>

<par>Either of the last two syllables could be assigned to etymon 288 <reconstruction>*b-raŋ</reconstruction> CHEST (cf. Qiang (Mawo) <latinform>ɹu qʰuaʴ</latinform> ‘chest’ ; Qiang (Taoping) <latinform>ʐu⁵⁵dzɿ³³qə³³</latinform> ‘chest’ ). The final syllable could also be assigned to 299 *t(s)u CHEST, if we allow an allofam with nasal final *t(s)u-N; it could also be assigned to 1276 <reconstruction>*kruːŋ CAGE/CHEST</reconstruction>, cf. Tujia <latinform>li⁵⁵kho⁵⁵tshoŋ⁵⁵</latinform>, <latinform>li⁵⁵ kʰo⁵⁵ tsʰũ⁵⁵</latinform> ‘chest’ .</par>
<par>First syllable is probably < Mand. Chinese <latinform>xiōng</latinform> ‘chest’ .</par>

<par>The first syllable <latinform>ʁua⁵⁵</latinform> may be descended from etymon 213 *r-gyap, but note that <latinform>dʑo³³lɑ³⁵</latinform> 'back' (182646) has the first syllable tagged 213. Cf. also 130978, which is the same Shixing form.</par>
<par>This form is rather arbitrarily assigned to this set, rather than to <xref ref="3586">#3586</xref>.</par>
<par>This is perhaps just a straight borrowing from Chinese <latinform>yèmdash aogǎn</latinform> 'waist, trunk, torso'.</par>
<par>It is not clear which of the two syllables descends from the present root, or whether some type of reduplication is involved.</par>

<par>Maru final <latinform>-k</latinform> is secondary; <latinform>-uk</latinform> is the regular Maru reflex of <reconstruction>*-əw</reconstruction>, as first noted in the original version of STC (p. 60 in the published version, 1972) and for the first time in print by Burling 1966 (<emph>Language</emph> 42.3). Thus this form is not to be referred to the stop-finalled allofam <xref ref="259">#259</xref>. Burling's correct observation about the secondariness of the velar stop in the Maru rhyme <latinform>-uk</latinform> (which is paralleled by the secondary <latinform>-t</latinform> in Maru <latinform>-it</latinform> < PTB <reconstruction>*-əy</reconstruction>), was attacked by Roy Andrew Miller "Once again, the Maru final stops" (1968 paper presented at ICSTLL #1, Yale University). This attack was in turn refuted in STC, in the new note 193 (added in 1972).</par>

<par>Qiangic shows evidence both for a nasal prefix (Ergong <latinform>mdʑo</latinform>, Ersu <latinform>xuɑi⁵⁵ntʂhɛ⁵⁵</latinform>) and prefixal <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> (Pumi Jinghua <latinform>stʃuɑ⁵⁵</latinform>).</par>
<par>Note the alternation between velar and labial initials, not surprising in view of the proto-labiovelar <reconstruction>*kʷ-</reconstruction> reconstructed for this root.</par>
